coredata.dc:description:
The lithium–oxygen (
Image 1001 ) battery offers an exceptional energy density but suffers from poor reversibility and electrolyte degradation due to large charge overpotentials. In this work, these challenges are addressed by introducing the ionic liquid crystal (ILC) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide,
Image 1002 , as a multifunctional additive in an electrolyte composed of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) with 0.100M lithium perchlorate (LiClO4). The ILC is a soluble catalyst that functions simultaneously as a stabilizing agent and redox mediator, reducing charge overpotentials and suppressing side reactions. This multifunction enhances catalytic activity and battery life. At a diluted ILC concentration of 0.035M and fixed capacity of 1000mA⋅h/g, the cyclability of the battery improves significantly over 50 cycles. In comparison, cells using DMSO/LiClO4 and with lithium bromide (LiBr) as redox mediator achieve only 18 and 7 cycles, respectively. In addition,
Image 1003 is observed to promote the protection of the Li anode through the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. To support these experimental findings, molecular dynamics and density functional theory simulations elucidate the species distribution and structure of the electrode–electrolyte interphase. In general, this study provides information on electrolyte engineering for
Image 1004 batteries, highlighting the potential of redox active, long-alkyl-chain additives in advanced energy-storage systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The lithium–oxygen (
Image 1001 ) battery offers an exceptional energy density but suffers from poor reversibility and electrolyte degradation due to large charge overpotentials. In this work, these challenges are addressed by introducing the ionic liquid crystal (ILC) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide,
Image 1002 , as a multifunctional additive in an electrolyte composed of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) with 0.100M lithium perchlorate (LiClO4). The ILC is a soluble catalyst that functions simultaneously as a stabilizing agent and redox mediator, reducing charge overpotentials and suppressing side reactions. This multifunction enhances catalytic activity and battery life. At a diluted ILC concentration of 0.035M and fixed capacity of 1000mA⋅h/g, the cyclability of the battery improves significantly over 50 cycles. In comparison, cells using DMSO/LiClO4 and with lithium bromide (LiBr) as redox mediator achieve only 18 and 7 cycles, respectively. In addition,
Image 1003 is observed to promote the protection of the Li anode through the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. To support these experimental findings, molecular dynamics and density functional theory simulations elucidate the species distribution and structure of the electrode–electrolyte interphase. In general, this study provides information on electrolyte engineering for
Image 1004 batteries, highlighting the potential of redox active, long-alkyl-chain additives in advanced energy-storage systems.

ce:section.ce:para:
Lithium–oxygen (
Image 1005 ) or lithium–air (
Image 1006 ) batteries are promising energy-storage technologies because of their high theoretical specific capacity and energy density [1,2]. In addition,
Image 1007 cells may offer cost advantages over conventional intercalation systems by reducing dependence on expensive transition metals such as Co, Ni, or Mn, which can account for up to half of the total cell cost [3].

Despite these advantages, the practical viability of
Image 1008 cells is hindered by side reactions and electrolyte degradation, particularly at high charging overpotentials [4,5]. These processes shorten cell lifetime, reduce reversibility, and limit implementation. Among the electrolyte systems explored, non-aqueous solvents such as dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and glymes have attracted interest due to their higher stability against superoxide and peroxide intermediates than traditional carbonate-based electrolytes [6,7]. Nevertheless, even these solvents degrade upon prolonged exposure to reactive oxygen species and high charging potentials [8,9].

To mitigate these limitations, two pragmatic strategies have been proposed: incorporating ionic liquids (ILs) and adding redox mediators (RMs) to the electrolyte [10]. ILs can provide a wide electrochemical stability window (ESW), negligible vapor pressure, and high thermal and chemical stability, making them attractive co-solvents for advanced electrolytes [11–14]. In contrast, RMs act as soluble catalysts that reduce charge and/or discharge overpotentials by transferring electrons to and from the discharge product Li2O2 at energetically favorable potentials [15,16]. Although both approaches have shown benefits when applied separately, their combined effect within a single electrolyte system remains underexplored. In particular, the design of multifunctional additives that simultaneously stabilize the electrolyte environment and mediate redox reactions is still under development.

Here, we address this gap by investigating, for the first time, the ionic liquid crystal (ILC) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide,
Image 1009 , as a multifunctional additive in the DMSO/LiClO4 electrolyte to improve
Image 1010 battery stability and cyclability. In this system, the bromide anion (
Image 1011 ) acts as an RM, while the
Image 1012 cation contributes to the formation of ionic nanodomains, which influence the ion solvation and mass/charge transport. Comparative studies indicate that LiBr-based electrolytes can be more effective in lowering charge overpotentials than ILC-based electrolytes, but often exhibit limited cyclability due to the corrosive nature of Br2 species. Furthermore, the
Image 1013 cation interacts strongly with the lithium metal anode, contributing to surface shielding and the formation of a solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer that improves cycling stability. To deepen our understanding of the
Image 1014 effect, we combine classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and density functional theory (DFT) calculations with experimental techniques to characterize the electrolyte structure and the distribution of species at the electrode–electrolyte interface. In general, this study introduces a strategy for incorporating long-alkyl-chain ILs with catalytic functionality into non-aqueous electrolytes for
Image 1015 cells, offering improved stability, cyclability, and cost efficiency by minimizing dependence on expensive transition-metal components.

To evaluate how ILC concentration affects
Image 1016 battery performance, we combined electrochemical measurements (cyclic voltammetry, deep discharge, galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)) with surface characterization (scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)) and computational modeling (classical molecular dynamics and periodic density functional theory calculations). The experimental and theoretical methodologies are described below.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Synthesis of
Image 1017 and preparation of the electrolytes
ce:section.ce:para:
The ionic liquid crystal 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide,
Image 1018 , was synthesized by our research group from 1-bromohexane (
Image 1019 , 98%, Sigma Aldrich), 1-methylimidazole (C4H6N2, ≥ 99%, Sigma Aldrich), and ethyl acetate (CH3COOC2H5, 100%, Synth) via a solvothermal process, as described previously [17]. The final product was dried and transferred to an argon-filled glovebox (MBRAUN-LABstar Workstation) for electrolyte preparation. Anhydrous dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ≥ 99.9%, Sigma-Aldrich) and lithium perchlorate (LiClO4, 99%, Sigma-Aldrich; 0.100M) were used to prepare the supporting electrolyte. The additive
Image 1020 was added at three concentrations: 0.035M, 0.350M, and 1.022M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CNT-ink-coated carbon paper electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
A CNT-based catalytic ink was prepared following a previously reported procedure [18]. Briefly, 25mg of commercial CNT (CNT CO. LTD), 4mL of distilled H2O, 1mL of isopropanol (
Image 1021 , > 99.5%, Synth), and 50µL of Nafion® (20%, Sigma Aldrich) were homogenized using an ultrasonic sonicator (Eco-Sonics). Subsequently, an 1mL aliquot of this suspension was further diluted with 400µL isopropanol and sonicated. The resulting low-viscosity ink was applied to both sides of Toray carbon paper (CP) disks (16.5mm diameter). The active-material loading per electrode was between 0.3 and 0.4mg.

All cell configurations were assembled in an argon-filled glovebox to minimize contamination. Electrolytes based on DMSO/LiClO4 (0.100M) containing different concentrations of the ionic liquid crystal (ILC) (0.035M, 0.350M, and 1.022M) were prepared for electrochemical measurements. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed under argon and oxygen atmospheres using a homemade three-electrode cell (see SI Figure S1) comprising a platinum counter electrode, a quasi-reference silver wire, and a glassy carbon working electrode coated with CNT ink. In addition, an electrolyte containing 0.035M LiBr in DMSO/LiClO4 was tested to compare the redox behavior of bromide species with that observed in the presence of
Image 1022 . CV measurements were recorded using a Multi Autolab/M204 potentiostat at a scan rate of 50mV/s. The voltage window was 2.0–6.5V vs. Li/
Image 1023 for all electrolyte formulations.

Deep-discharge and galvanostatic charge–discharge tests were conducted in a custom-made Swagelok-type cell, as previously described [18]. Lithium-metal disks (99.9%, Tob Machine) served as anodes, and CNT-ink-coated carbon paper electrodes were used as cathodes. A glass-fiber membrane (GF50/A, HNM), wetted with 100µL electrolyte, was placed between the electrodes. The assembled cells were stored in the glovebox for three days to stabilize before testing. Measurements were performed using a Multi Autolab/M204 potentiostat in chronopotentiometry mode. Cells were operated in O2 at 3bar in a closed system. For deep-discharge measurements, a constant current of 0.050mA was applied with a discharge cutoff of 2.2V. For cycling tests, the cutoff potential was set between 2.2V and 4.5V, with a fixed current of 0.050mA and a fixed capacity of 1000mA⋅h/g. Additional cycling tests were conducted using five-discharge and four-charge conditions to evaluate the influence of
Image 1024 on lithium-anode modification. A
Image 1025 cell using DMSO/LiClO4 (0.100M) with 0.050M LiBr (a formulation frequently reported in the literature [19]) was also tested under GCD conditions. This system served as a reference to compare the effects of LiBr with those of
Image 1026 on cell cyclability.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements were performed in Li/Li symmetrical cells pressurized with argon and oxygen (3bar) using the electrolyte compositions of 0.035M and 1.022M ILC, 0.050M LiBr, and the neat electrolyte (DMSO/LiClO4 0.100M without additive). The tests were conducted on the PARSTAT 3000A-DX bipotentiostat using a two-electrode configuration, in which the counter and reference electrodes were connected to the anode current collector and the sensor and working electrodes were connected to the cathode current collector. The impedance spectra were recorded before and after chronoamperometry in the frequency range of 1MHz to 100mHz, applying an AC voltage amplitude of 10mV.

Scanning electron microscopy was employed to examine the electrode morphology and/or product formation after both the deep discharge test and the five-discharge/four-charge cycling tests. A Thermo Scientific Quattro S microscope equipped with an Everhart–Thornley Detector (ETD) was used for imaging. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were carried out using a Thermo Scientific K-ALPHA (Al) model to determine the chemical composition and oxidation states of the species formed at the anode and to assess the possible formation of a solid electrolyte interphase on the lithium surface.

To better understand how the
Image 1027 affects the electrode–electrolyte interphase structure in the charged and discharged DMSO/ LiClO4 0.100M
Image 1028 cells, we performed classical molecular dynamics simulations for the electrolyte confined between a carbon-based electrode and a lithium-metal electrode. To capture the behavior of ILC in both dilute and concentrated regimes, we considered two concentrations, 0.200M and 1.456M. Although these values differ from those used experimentally, they were selected to balance statistical accuracy and computational cost in the CMD simulations. As well as to keep consistency with our previous work [17].

To model the effect of the charge in the battery, we considered three electrode surface charge densities;
( i )
zero total charges (discharged battery),
( i i )
± 0.1570e/nm2 (intermediate charge), and
( i i i )
± 0.4997e/nm2 (fully charged). These electrode surface charge densities were selected based on established literature values for electrode–electrolyte simulations with carbon-based electrodes [20,21]. To model the charged electrodes, we manually added partial charges to the atoms in the first layer of the electrodes in direct contact with the electrolyte [21]. Positive and negative charges were added to the carbon-based and lithium metal-based electrodes, respectively. The total charges were balanced between the electrodes to maintain a neutral simulation box and to ensure equal charge density across both electrodes. For the partial charge values used in each simulation, see the SI, Table S8.

For the
Image 1029 ILC and DMSO molecules we employed the CL&P force field developed by Canongia Lopes and Pádua [22]. For the DMSO molecules we used the atomic charges obtained by Strade and Feller [23]. For
Image 1030 we adopted the parameters from Acevedo et al. [24] while for
Image 1031 we used the default parameters of OPLS [25]. It is worth highlighting that all the force fields used are based on the OPLS parameters, which ensures the compatibility between them. Furthermore, these force fields and also the relaxed structures used in the MD simulations have already been used in previous work [17].

The electrodes were simulated considering only intermolecular interactions, Lennard-Jones and Coulombic potential. For the carbon-based we used the OPLS σ and ϵ parameters common used for benzene structure, [26] while for the lithium-metal we used the force field developed by Costa et al. [27] for the metallic
Image 1032 . Our carbon-based electrode is composed by three graphene layers spaced by 0.3354nm with the ABA packing, similarly to the graphite structure, with the x and y -axis having respectively 6.6447nm and 5.9654nm, then, each graphene layer has a total of 1512 atoms. For the Li metal electrode, the structure was built based on the 100 surface configuration, however, to have a perfect match with the carbon-based electrode, a small strain of -0.365% and -0.028% was added to the x and y -axis, respectively. The Li electrode is made up of 14 layers, each with 323 atoms. Both electrodes are kept frozen in their initial positions in all the simulations, and the interactions between them are null. More details of the electrode models are provided in the SI.

Performing CMD simulations for confined electrode–electrolyte systems requires careful protocols to ensure correct simulation-box density and potential energy convergence. Here we employed a two-block simulation protocol based on the approach previously reported by Heuer and coworkers [20,28]. First, the simulation boxes are built using the PACKMOL software [29] with densities based in our previous work [17]. Then, 10-4 Lennard-Jones potential walls are added at the z -axis boundaries to model the interface and the initial structures are relaxed using the steepest descent and conjugated algorithm followed by short NVT simulation at 600K. After, the simulation boxes densities are obtained by NpT simulations, in which only the z -axis can be compressed.

Subsequently, the potential walls are replaced by the atomistic electrode structures and the initial structures are relaxed again, then, a 1400ns NVT simulation at 400K is performed in which the last 600ns are considered production data. For the simulations of charged electrodes, the first configuration 100ns in the equilibration process for each system was selected and heated to 500K. Then, the partial charges in the electrodes are manually increased and a 10ns NVT simulation is performed. The previous process was repeated several times until the desired electrode charge was reached and a cooling simulation was performed. Finally, a 1400ns NVT simulation is performed at 400K and the last 600ns are used to calculate structural properties.

In all MD simulations, the temperature was controlled using the v-rescale thermostat with a coupling time of 0.1ps, the smooth particle mesh Ewald method with a 1.6nm cutoff was used to handle the Coulombic interactions, while for the van der Waals, only the cutoff was used. For the simulations containing the atomic electrode structure we applied a vacuum along the z -axes with twice the size of the simulation box as required by the 3dc Ewald correction [30] to avoid interactions. The equation of motions were integrated every 0.002ps using the leapfrog algorithm. As suggested for OPLS-based force fields, the
Image 1033 bonds were constrained using the LINCS algorithm. In the Lennard-Jones walls only
x y
periodic images were used and the c-rescale barostat was used to control pressure in the NpT simulations. All the simulations were performed using GROMACS package version 20224.2

The ab initio calculations are based on DFT [31,32] framework within the semilocal Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof [33] (PBE) exchange–correlation energy functional, as implemented in the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP), version 5.4.4 [34,35]. To improve the description of the long-range nonlocal van der Waals (vdW) interactions, [36,37] we used the semi-empirical D3 correction [38]. The Kohn–Sham states were represented through an expansion in plane waves, and the projector augmented wave (PAW) method was used to characterize the interactions between the core and valence electrons [39,40]. The equilibrium lattice of the body-centered cubic (bcc) bulk Li was obtained by minimizing the stress tensor, while a plane wave cutoff energy of 488.734eV, which is 12.5% higher than the maximum recommended cutoff energy for the selected PAW projectors.

The Brillouin zone (BZ) integration was carried out using a Monkhorst–Pack [41] k-mesh of
10 × 10 × 10
for the Li bulk. The
Image 1034 surface, which is the most stable surface [42], was described by the repeated slab geometry. We used the theoretical equilibrium lattice constant of 3.39Å to construct a
5 × 5
supercell with five atomic layers and a vacuum thickness of 15Å and the two bottom atomic layers were kept frozen. The ionic liquid
Image 1035 and the DMSO molecule total energy calculations were performed using a cubic box of 20Å. For all calculations involving the slab model and the gas-phase molecules, only the Γ -point was used to integrate the BZ. All equilibrium geometries were obtained when all residual forces in all atoms were less than 0.05eV/Å, using an energy self-consistency criterion of 1 × 10-5 eV.

We integrate experimental measurements with theoretical computations to improve our understanding of the correlation between ILC concentration and
Image 1036 battery performance. Subsequently, we delineated the findings in a sequential manner.

Cyclic voltammetry tests were conducted to validate the redox capacity of the additive
Image 1038 and to understand the differences between the oxidation and reduction processes in inert and oxidative atmospheres. Fig. 1 shows the CV results using different electrolyte compositions, i.e., DMSO/LiClO4 with 0.035M ILC, 0.350M ILC and 0.035M LiBr. The left side of Fig. 1 ( a to c ) indicates the results using argon, while the medium side ( d to f ) represents the results using O2. Due to the high electrochemical stability window of the ILC additive, CV scans were carried out in the wide potential range of 2.0 to 6.5V vs. Li/
Image 1039 .

For both LiBr and ILC systems, the presence of redox peaks is evidenced in the argon atmosphere, which confirms that the counterion of the ILC,
Image 1040 , has an RM effect comparable to LiBr. In the literature, RMs are used to reduce the charge and/or discharge overpotentials of batteries, improving the electrode and electrolyte stabilities and prolonging cell operation time [43–45]. Additionally, oxidizing a soluble element surpasses more complex surface–surface reactions, thus facilitating the degradation of
Image 1041 and improving cyclability. In
Image 1042 batteries, the
Image 1043 ion acts as an RM during charging, forming two reversible redox couples:
Image 1044 /
Image 1045 and
Image 1046 /Br2, as indicated in Eqs. (5) and (6), respectively [46,47].

To compare the redox behavior of
Image 1047 and LiBr, CV measurements were performed using
Image 1048 /DMSO electrolytes containing 0.035M ILC and 0.035M LiBr, ensuring the same concentration of bromide anions. A displacement in the redox peaks of
Image 1049 compared to LiBr was observed in the inert atmosphere, which could be due to the presence of the imidazolium cation in the ILC-based system. Previously, Torriero and Howlett reported that the solvation properties of ionic liquids formed by different cationic and anionic groups as well as under varying concentrations could influence on the redox potentials of the electrolyte, as indicated by CV measurements [48]. In our study, the oxidation/reduction of the first redox pair
Image 1050 /
Image 1051 was observed at ≈ 4.0V/3.75V for
Image 1052 , and at ≈ 4.1V/3.85V for LiBr electrolyte. The second
Image 1053 /Br2 redox couple was observed at potentials higher than 4.5V for both
Image 1054 and LiBr, while the reduction peaks could not be easily observed.

It is important to note that for the operation of
Image 1055 cells, the formation of the first
Image 1056 /
Image 1057 couple is more desirable, as it is formed at lower potentials than
Image 1058 /Br2. In addition, the Br2 species are highly reactive and can cause degradation of the organic solvent and attack the electrodes [15]. Based on this statement, the electrolyte compositions presented in this study indicate an advantage for
Image 1059 cells because the corrosive Br2 is not easily formed in the cycling window of this device.

When CV was performed under O2 atmosphere, small shifts in oxidation potentials of
Image 1060 /
Image 1061 ( ≈ 4.00V) and
Image 1062 / Br2 ( ≈ 4.50V) were observed for the three electrolyte compositions. Furthermore, two anodic peaks were also observed for 0.035M LiBr at 2.80V and 3.40V, while for 0.035M ILC, peaks were observed at 2.72V and 3.44V. These peaks are attributed to the oxidation of
Image 1063 (Eq. (1)) and
Image 1064 species (Eq. (2)), respectively, comparable to the works of Belova et al. [49] and Dilimon et al. [50] who investigated similar electrolyte compositions (0.100M
Image 1065 /DMSO) using glassy carbon as a working electrode.
(1)
(2)

For 0.350M ILC under O2 atmosphere, only one anodic peak was observed at 2.73V, which is attributed to
Image 1068 oxidation. Analyzing the cathodic scan, a peak was observed at 2.65V for 0.035M LiBr, 2.68V for 0.035M ILC and 2.77V for 0.350M ILC. These peaks can be related to the formation of
Image 1069 or the reduction of
Image 1070 to Li2O2, as shown in Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively. The peak identified for the reduction of
Image 1071 shows a shift compared to that observed by Belova et al. [49] in 2.50V, which could be caused by the presence of
Image 1072 .
(3)
(4)

Finally, it can also be noted for the 0.035M ILC electrolyte, a broadening of the
Image 1075 to
Image 1076 oxidation peak when compared to the one observed in Ar atmosphere, which might indicate that the species
Image 1077 formed from the electrochemical oxidation of
Image 1078 (see Eq. (5)) oxidizes the product Li2O2 to
Image 1079 and O2, while
Image 1080 is reduced back to
Image 1081 (Eq. (7)) [47]. Therefore, this process facilitates the reversibility of
Image 1082 cells and decreases its charge overpotentials, by oxidizing the Li2O2 product at potentials close to those of RM, reducing electrolyte attack and prolonging cell cyclability [15].
(5)
(6)
(7)

Cycling tests were carried out at a fixed capacity of 1000mA⋅h/g, a current density of 125mA/g, and cutoff potentials from 2.2V to 4.5V, to assess the effect of the
Image 1086 additive into the
Image 1087 cell lifetime. The cycling results showed a pattern different from that initially expected for a high-conductive system using ILC. Typically, the number of cycles is associated with the battery’s discharge capacity, with the expectation of longer cyclability for cells that present superior capacity [51]. However, this trend is not observed in Fig. 1
g and h . Based on the results of the deep discharge, Fig. 1
g , it is observed that the gravimetric discharge capacity of each electrolyte condition is given in the following descending order: 0.035M > 0.000M > 1.022M. Therefore, a limited cycling performance should be expected for a high ILC concentration. In fact, the electrolyte composition that presented the longest cyclability was 0.035M ILC with more than 50 cycles. However, concentrated 1.022M ILC delivered better cycling performance than neat electrolyte, 0.000M ILC, with 21 and 18 cycles, respectively.

The results found in deep discharge could be assigned to the viscous nature of the ILC, which hinders the mass transport of species in the electrolyte [17], limiting the discharge capacity. This viscosity effect is generally evident in highly concentrated IL systems [17,52,53]. For example, our previous work shows that at 290.15K, the viscosity of DMSO/LiClO4 0.100M with a 1.022M
Image 1088 concentration is nearly three times greater than that observed for the 0.035M, which is due to the strong Coulombic interactions between the ILC ions and also to the
Image 1089 tendency to form ionic nanodomains [17,54]. Furthermore, the classical MD simulations from our previous work also highlight the slowing down of the mass and charge transport in the electrolyte when the ILC concentration increases, which is directly related to the formation of the ionic nanodomains and the increase in viscosity [17].

The addition of ILs to the electrolyte has also been reported to alter its solvation properties, supported by Lewis acid–base chemistry [55,56], in which the ionic liquid cation
Image 1090 is a weak acid that stabilizes the discharge intermediate
Image 1091 , weak base, while the strong acid
Image 1092 associates with anion
Image 1093 and forms a strong base
Image 1094 , leading to the appearance of large Li2O2 species. This theory is confirmed by the change in the morphology and characteristics of the Li2O2 products, SI Fig. 2, where the conventional DMSO/LiClO4 system forms particles of different sizes without defined shape, while the diluted 0.035M ILC electrolyte forms a type of Li2O2 film with a few Li2O2 particles, and the concentrated 1.022M ILC forms a more uniform film with circular particles (toroids).

Regarding the cycling profile, Fig. 1
h , the addition of a low concentration of ILC to the electrolyte significantly improves cell performance, while the highly concentrated system showed a subtle improvement. We suggest that this enhancement is attributed to the multifunctional role of the proposed
Image 1095 additive, particularly its superior ESW and chemical resistance against superoxide species compared to the pure DMSO, and its role as RM. Knipping et al. [57] investigated the impact of ILs with different functional groups and structures on the performance of
Image 1096 cells, and found that cell specific capacity was enhanced by proper lithium solvation, ionic conductivity, and viscosity, but other factors could also influence cyclability, including the electrolyte ESW and its decomposition. These findings support our hypothesis that the joint properties of the ILC contribute to improve the cell operation, but excess addition of the ILC can affect the transport properties.

Fig. 1
i illustrates the first cycle of
Image 1097 cells under each electrolyte condition. The DMSO/LiClO4 system exhibits a charging potential that exceeds 4.2V, which is the value at which DMSO degradation initiates [58]. Furthermore, previous studies have demonstrated that DMSO decomposes in the presence of the Li2O2 product, which has a nucleophilic character [59,60]. Therefore, the incorporation of a multifunctional ionic liquid into an aprotic organic solvent has been beneficial for the overall stability of the system. In particular, even a small concentration of
Image 1098 , such as 0.035M, effectively reduces the charging overpotentials to below 4.2V, demonstrating the efficacy of the
Image 1099 counterion as a RM. Increasing the concentration of ILC to 1.022M results in a higher availability of
Image 1100 anions to degrade the discharge product Li2O2, leading to even lower charging overpotentials. Furthermore, it was previously reported that the strong interactions between
Image 1101 cation and DMSO molecule enhance the stability of the organic solvent and mitigate its attack [17].

Since many authors have investigated the inherent capacity of the LiBr additive to reduce the charge overpotentials of various organic electrolytes, a battery composed of DMSO/LiClO4 0.100M and 0.050M LiBr electrolyte was assembled and cycled under the same conditions as the previous batteries. This concentration of LiBr was chosen based on a previous work reported by Cremasco et al. [19]. By analyzing the first complete cycle of the
Image 1102 battery, Fig. 1
i , it is observed that the LiBr salt acts as a much more effective redox mediator than
Image 1103 , being comparable to the concentrated ILC system, which has a much higher concentration of bromide anions. The ILC, on the other hand, has a large cationic structure that alters the solvation properties of the system, which could increase resistance in the medium and imply less accessibility of the bromide anions to decompose the Li2O2 product [17]. However, after further cycling the battery with LiBr-based electrolyte, it was observed that the system reached the cutoff discharge potential rapidly, during the 7th cycle. Although cell operation continued, significant instabilities emerged in the following cycles (SI Figure S3).

After cell cycling, the batteries were disassembled in the glovebox. The Li anode in the LiBr-based electrolyte was found to be highly degraded (See SI Figure S4), while the Li anode used in ILC-based systems exhibited significantly less decomposition, especially for the concentrated ILC condition. This is evidence that in addition to stabilizing the DMSO electrolyte, the
Image 1104 cation also contributes to the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase layer, improving the protection of the lithium anode. Therefore, the ILC additive has shown superior performance compared to the LiBr.

To obtain information regarding the effect of the ILC addition in the formation of a SEI layer and the morphology of the electrode–electrolyte interphase, first we performed five electrochemical cycles with a current of 0.050mA and a fixed capacity of 1000mA⋅h/g for
Image 1105 cells containing electrolyte compositions of 0.000M, 0.035M, and 1.022M ILC (SI Figure S5). The number of cycles was defined to ensure, whether formed, the presence of the solid electrolyte interphase on the surface of the Li anode. After cycling tests, the electrodes were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. These techniques provided valuable information on how different electrolyte compositions influenced the Li metal, highlighting the intriguing relationship between concentration levels, decomposition tendencies, and SEI formation.

Scanning electron microscopy analyses, Fig. 2
a , show several discernible decomposition patterns on the surface of the cathode and anode, depending on the electrolyte composition. In the neat electrolyte, that is, without the ILC additive, the anode exhibited accelerated degradation, as evidenced by the pronounced surface irregularities and notable morphological changes [60]. The addition of ILC results in the formation of a protective SEI layer. At the highest ILC concentration of 1.022M, the layer exhibits a considerably thicker and smoother morphology, as highlighted within the yellow rectangles in the image. This difference may be attributed to the Li surface shielding promoted by the ILC addition, while in the neat electrolyte the anode is attacked due to its direct contact with the DMSO molecule. Previous works have also demonstrated the influence of ionic liquids in promoting the formation of a stable and uniform SEI layer with reduced dendrite growth [61–64]. Besides, these observations were confirmed by XPS data, which provided critical insights into the chemical composition and stability of
Image 1113 batteries, particularly with respect to the formation of Li species and the impact of varying the ILC concentrations.

Fig. 2
b shows the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses of the lithium anode after 5 cycles at different ILC concentrations. The analysis of the Li
1 s
spectrum reveals that changes in the concentration of ILC significantly influence the formation of lithium products. At higher concentrations of ILCs, fewer degradation products are detected, indicating a stabilizing effect on lithium chemistry. A similar trend is observed when analyzing the S
2 p
spectrum, which provides information about sulfur-containing species involved in the electrolyte, which are the prominent peaks for the
Image 1114 and
Image 1115 bonds, with the sulfur element originating from DMSO.

The XPS data, Fig. 2
b , demonstrate that at higher ILC concentrations, the decomposition of the electrolyte is minimized. This is a key finding, as sulfur-based species are often indicative of degradation in
Image 1116 battery electrolytes. In contrast, in 0.000M ILC (neat electrolyte), electrolyte degradation is much more pronounced, as evidenced by the substantial increase in sulfur-based decomposition products. The carbon spectra confirm these observations, showing that high concentrations of ILC prevent the formation of unwanted byproducts and highlight that by improving the ILC concentration, only the band related to the
Image 1117 bonds appears. This behavior shows evidence that
Image 1118 will be deposited on the lithium surface. Furthermore, the absence of significant carbonaceous degradation products in the XPS analysis suggests that ILCs play a protective role, preserving the integrity of the electrolyte. Therefore, the addition of
Image 1119 to the electrolyte delayed the attack on metallic lithium, in agreement with what is observed in SEM images of the diluted and concentrated electrolytes, i.e., 0.035M and 1.022M ILC, respectively. It is also notable that in the 1.022M ILC system, the Li anode demonstrated reduced levels of degradation, exhibited minimal surface disruptions, and preserved its structural integrity to a greater degree. Taking this into account, it appears that lithium-metal shielding is correlated with the ILC concentration, as the higher content of
Image 1120 in the system provides a thicker protective layer on the surface of the Li anode, implying fewer attacks on the anode during cell operation.

The XPS spectra in Fig. 2
b also show chemically reasonable binding energies for the main
Image 1121 interphase species, and the fitted peak positions match the values in the literature within typical reference uncertainty. In the Li
1 s
region, components assigned to
Image 1122 /LiOH/ and
Image 1123 are expected around 55–56eV, with small chemical shifts that are best validated via their corresponding O
1 s
and C
1 s
partners rather than absolute energy alone. The O
1 s
envelope is dominated by lattice oxygen from
Image 1124 /LiOH/
Image 1125 near 529.5–531eV and a higher-energy contribution at ≈ 531.5eV from carbonate-type oxygen in
Image 1126 and organic carbonates, consistent with reference spectra for native Li surfaces and SEI films.

In the C
1 s
spectra, the main hydrocarbon peak is correctly set at approximately 284.8eV and the carbonate peak for Li2CO3 and
Image 1127 appears at 289–290eV, with intermediate
Image 1128 /
Image 1129 components between 286–288eV attributed to DMSO-derived organics and
Image 1130 SEI species. The S
2 p
doublets located in the 168–170eV range indicate oxidized sulfur species such as sulfites/sulfates formed from anion decomposition, while the Br
3 d
doublets observed near 68–69eV are consistent with bromide and
Image 1131 environments in imidazolium bromide salts and their surface complexes in ionic-liquid-based electrolytes [65–67].

Notably, SEM imagery and XPS data highlighted the pivotal role of SEI in safeguarding lithium-metal anodes from detrimental electrolyte reactions and corrosive processes. It became evident that higher concentrations of ILC-based electrolytes facilitated the development of a robust and more uniform SEI layer, serving as a barrier against electrolyte infiltration and mitigating the risks of electrolyte decomposition. The variation in thickness and uniformity of the SEI layer between the different concentrations underscored the crucial influence of electrolyte concentration on the quality and efficacy of SEI protection.

To support these results, EIS measurements were performed on Li/Li symmetric cells under inert and oxidative atmospheres. The EIS spectra, Fig. 2
c , exhibit a high-frequency intercept on the real impedance axis (Z’), corresponding to the ohmic resistance, and a semicircle associated with the charge-transfer resistance [68].

Under an argon atmosphere, the spectra reveal distinct SEI characteristics across the ILC-based and neat electrolytes. The largest charge-transfer resistance (largest semicircle) is observed for the highest ILC concentration, which is indicative of a thick, robust passivation layer that effectively shields Li metal from oxidation during long-term cycling [69]. In contrast, the neat electrolyte, which lacks both ILC and LiBr additives, exhibits lower charge-transfer resistance but also less stable interfacial behavior, highlighting the importance of ILC additives in forming self-limiting and durable SEI layers that balance ionic conductivity and long-term passivation.

These findings are consistent with SEM and XPS analyses, which show that higher concentrations of
Image 1132 promote the formation of a thicker and more uniform protective layer on the Li surface, whereas the more diluted electrolyte (0.035M) leads to a thinner interphase with lower resistance. An increase in resistance is observed after chronoamperometry for diluted and concentrated ILC systems, indicating a possible structural reorganization of the ILC near the Li surface, leading to a thicker SEI. The LiBr-based system displays a smaller, initially low-resistance interphase due to conductive bromide species; however, this interphase is less stable and can degrade under oxidative conditions or prolonged cycling, risking progressive Li exposure and poor long-term performance, in agreement with cell-performance tests and previously published results [19].

Under an oxygen atmosphere, all systems show an increase in charge-transfer resistance, with the diluted ILC electrolyte (0.035M) approaching the behavior of the LiBr-based system. In particular, the impedance spectra of the ILC-based electrolyte curves develop a characteristic shoulder at 200–250 Ω , signaling a stratified SEI with compact inner and outer porous layers
Image 1133 as shown in the XPS results for improved oxidative resilience. This multilayered structure improves oxidative stability while allowing selective transport of
Image 1134 , optimal for oxygen-tolerant Li interfaces in different environments [70]. The highest concentration investigated in this work (1.022M) was primarily explored to investigate the upper limit of the effects of ionic liquid crystal structure on electrolyte and electrode–electrolyte interphase. This composition is not intended to represent an optimized practical electrolyte formulation but rather to provide mechanistic insight into how increasing ILC content influences nanodomain formation, interfacial organization, and SEI stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomistic characterization of the additive effect
ce:section.ce:para:
This section focuses on resolving the composition and structure of the electrode–electrolyte interphase to clarify how electrolyte species are redistributed during battery operation, and how these changes may affect reactivity and cycling behavior. Classical molecular dynamics simulations were performed for the confined DMSO/LiClO4 0.100M electrolyte at two ILC concentrations, 0.200M and 1.456M, and at three electrode charge sets:
( i )
zero total charge (discharged battery),
( i i )
± 0.1570e/nm2 (intermediate charge), and
( i i i )
± 0.4997e/nm2 (fully charged). As stated above, this ILC concentration was chosen on the basis of a previous publication and to maintain affordable computational costs and reasonable statistics [17].

The number density profile of particles in the z -axis direction (orthogonal to the electrodes), Fig. 3, shows a pronounced tendency for the ions to occupy the electrode–electrolyte interphase region in the discharged battery (cyan curves) at both concentrations. As ILC concentration increases from 0.200M to 1.456M, the ion populations in the interphase regions increase, leading to a low DMSO population in the region as a consequence of the large sterical hindrance in the region caused by the large
Image 1140 .

The pronounced preference of ions for the interfacial region can be explained by the joining of two main factors; first, because of the flat shape of the imidazolium ring, the ILC tends to orient itself with the ring parallel to the electrode surface, [20] which improves the contact area and the van der Waals interactions between the electrode and the ions atoms. Second, due to its large alkyl chains and ionic character, the
Image 1141 cation tend to form ionic nanodomains [71,72] with polar and nonpolar regions organized in structures that resemble micelles, then, as soon as the cations occupy the electrode–electrolyte interfacial regions, the other ions are pulled to these regions, and the domains are formed. Fig. 4
a shows snapshots of MD simulations in which the
Image 1142 are highlighted in blue and the structure of the nanodomains can be seen in the discharged battery simulation.

When the electrodes are charged, that is, positive and negative charges are added to the carbon-based and lithium-metal electrodes, respectively, the electrolyte species redistribute themselves inside the simulation box to counterbalance the charge effects of the electrode surfaces. As expected, as the charge magnitude increases, there is a decrease of the
Image 1143 population at the positive electrode (carbon) interface while increasing its concentration close to the negative electrode (lithium). The
Image 1144 ions exhibit the opposite behavior: their population decreases at the negative electrode and increases at the carbon-based electrode. Unlike
Image 1145 ,
Image 1146 , and
Image 1147 , which redistribute themselves to counterbalance electrode surface charges, the
Image 1148 cations tend to accumulate in the both electrode interface region due to the strong
Image 1149 interactions. Furthermore, the fully charged battery displays a high concentration of DMSO molecules at the positive electrode interface, which can be mainly attributed to the reduced steric hindrance in the region due to the migration of the bulky
Image 1150 cation away from the region. The observed behaviors are consistent with the experimental results, suggesting that the accumulation of
Image 1151 cations at the Li anode surface forms a protective interfacial layer that mitigates Li degradation induced by DMSO and its decomposition byproducts.

Finally, in order to explore in depth the interaction of
Image 1152 and DMSO with the lithium surface, DFT calculations were performed. Due to computational cost, the
Image 1153 ILC was emulated by the
Image 1154 pair. The interaction between IL ions and the lithium surface is stronger than the adsorption of DMSO, which agrees with the tendency of the ions to occupy the interfacial regions in MD simulations, Fig. 3. The adsorption energy was calculated as follows:

Image 1158 is -2.85eV, while that of DMSO is -1.16eV.

Fig. 4 illustrates the electronic density difference between the surface and the adsorbates calculated as follows:

Image 1164 represent the electronic densities of the total system, the isolated molecule (DMSO or
Image 1165 ) in its adsorbed position, and the frozen substrate, respectively. The ionic liquid exhibits a higher charge transfer between the surface and the adsorbate, along with a greater surface layer distortion. Table 1 presents the effective density derived electrostatic and chemical [73,74] charges (DDEC) for the adsorbates and for each atomic layer of the surface. The stronger interaction between the ionic liquid and the lithium surface, compared to DMSO, is further demonstrated by the magnitude of the charge variation when DMSO or
Image 1166 is adsorbed. Then, when the DFT and MD simulation results are merged, it is clear that the IL has a stronger interaction with the lithium-metal electrode, implying its shielding effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational and experimental insights into the ILC effect
ce:section.ce:para:
The combined molecular dynamics and density functional theory analyses provide a consistent description of the interfacial organization and energetics that govern electrolyte–electrode interactions. The MD results reveal a strong spatial segregation of ionic species and solvent molecules across the electrochemical cell, with ions preferentially occupying the electrode–electrolyte interface due to the strong interactions between
Image 1167 cations and the electrode surface and its tendency to form structured nanodomains. This high interfacial concentration of
Image 1168 leads to a large steric hindrance, which consequently displaces a large population of DMSO molecules into the bulk electrolyte region.

Upon polarization, the imidazolium cations accumulate at the lithium electrode to counterbalance the charges, consistent with experimental evidence indicating the involvement of ionic liquid species in the formation of the solid–electrolyte interphase. The DFT calculations further substantiate these observations, demonstrating that the adsorption of
Image 1169 on Li(100) is stronger than that of DMSO, accompanied by higher charge transfer and greater surface layer distortion. These results confirm the stronger affinity of the ionic liquid toward the metallic lithium surface. The agreement between atomistic simulations and experimental trends highlights the dominant role of ionic liquid cations in initiating SEI formation, with DMSO molecules likely occupying residual interfacial spaces and contributing to secondary solvation structures. Together, the combined computational and experimental insights delineate the molecular origins of interphase composition and stability in ionic-liquid-based lithium electrolyte systems.

From the electrochemical analysis, we can clearly see the impact of adding ILC on
Image 1170 battery performance. This effect arises from the presence of bromide anions and the suppression of Br2 formation due to the low overpotentials observed in cycling tests, which can increase the battery lifespan. Although LiBr reduces the initial charging overpotential more effectively, its long-term cycling stability is limited by severe degradation of the lithium metal anode. In experiments, cells containing LiBr exhibited only 7 cycles, whereas the electrolyte containing 0.035M
Image 1171 sustained more than 50 cycles, corresponding to approximately a seven-fold improvement in cycle life.

This difference arises from the multifunctional character of the ionic liquid crystal additive. While LiBr acts solely as a redox mediator,
Image 1172 cation simultaneously stabilizes the lithium interface by promoting the formation of a protective SEI layer, as confirmed by SEM, XPS, and EIS analyses. Furthermore, DFT calculations reveal that the ionic liquid exhibits a significantly stronger interaction with the lithium surface (adsorption energy -2.85eV) than DMSO (-1.16eV), indicating preferential adsorption of imidazolium species at the anode interface. This interfacial structuring reduces the direct solvent–lithium contact and suppresses parasitic reactions, thereby preserving the lithium anode during repeated cycling. As a result, ILC combines catalytic redox mediation with interfacial stabilization, enabling substantially improved long-term cyclability compared to conventional LiBr additives.

The concentration of the ionic liquid crystal plays a critical role in balancing interfacial stability and mass transport in
Image 1173 batteries. At low concentrations (0.035M), the
Image 1174 additive provides effective redox mediation and lithium surface stabilization while preserving the favorable transport properties of the DMSO-based electrolyte. Under these conditions, the increase in viscosity is minimal, allowing efficient diffusion of
Image 1175 ions and dissolution of oxygen species within the porous cathode structure. As the ILC concentration increases, stronger interfacial structuring and improved lithium protection are observed, as confirmed by SEM, XPS, and EIS analyses showing the formation of a thicker and more uniform SEI layer. However, excessive concentrations lead to the formation of ionic nanodomains and increased viscosity, which reduce ionic mobility and oxygen transport in the electrolyte, as highlighted by the EIS data. These transport limitations increase internal resistance and ultimately hinder the electrochemical performance of the
Image 1176 battery. Therefore, the multifunctionality of the ILC additive combines redox mediation, lithium anode protection, catalytic improvement, and electrochemical stability, presenting a promising approach to address the key limitations of
Image 1177 batteries. However, optimal electrolyte design is required to balance the interfacial stabilization provided by the ionic liquid crystal with the transport requirements necessary for efficient cell operation.

This study demonstrates the promising role of the multifunctional additive
Image 1178 in improving the performance of
Image 1179 batteries using an aprotic electrolyte DMSO /
Image 1180 0.100M. By combining key features of conventional ionic liquids with structural and redox-mediator properties, the additive facilitates Li2O2 decomposition during recharge. The presence of
Image 1181 reduces charge overpotentials, suppresses the formation of corrosive species such as Br2, and thereby lowers electrolyte degradation, prolonging cell lifetime. Cyclic voltammetry confirms redox activity through the
Image 1182 /
Image 1183 and
Image 1184 /Br2 couples. The best performance was achieved at 0.035M
Image 1185 , where the balance between redox mediation, cationic protection of the Li anode, and favorable ionic mobility (due to lower viscosity) enables superior cycling stability. In contrast, higher concentrations (e.g., 1.022M) lead to nanodomain formation, reduced ionic transport, and increased resistance, hindering battery performance. Moreover, the imidazolium-based ionic liquid crystal promotes the formation of a stable SEI on the Li-metal anode, mitigating degradation.

These results suggest that relatively low concentrations of the ILC additive provide the most favorable compromise between catalytic activity, interfacial stabilization, and electrolyte transport properties, highlighting the importance of carefully optimizing additive concentration for practical
Image 1186 battery operation.
